message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Data describing the early additional protection afforded by recently recommended XBB1.5- adapted COVID-19 vaccines are limited.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We estimated the association between receipt of BNT162b2 XBB1.5-adapted vaccine (Pfizer- BioNTech 2023–2024 formulation) and medically attended COVID-19 outcomes among adults ≥18 years of age.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design, Setting, and Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We performed a test-negative case-control study to compare the odds of BNT162b2 XBB1.5- adapted vaccine receipt between COVID-19 cases and test-negative controls among adults in the Kaiser Permanente Southern California health system between October 11 and December 10, 2023. Adjusted odds ratios (OR) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) were estimated from multivariable logistic regression models that were adjusted for patient demographic and clinical characteristics.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposure</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The primary exposure was receipt of BNT162b2 XBB1.5-adapted vaccine compared to not receiving an XBB1.5-adapted vaccine of any kind, regardless of prior COVID-19 vaccination or SARS-CoV-2 infection history. We also compared receipt of prior (non-XBB1.5-adapted) versions of COVID-19 vaccines to the unvaccinated to estimate remaining protection from older vaccines.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcomes and Measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Cases were those with a positive SARS-CoV-2 polymerase chain reaction test, and controls tested negative. Analyses were done separately for COVID-19 hospital admissions, emergency department (ED) and urgent care (UC) encounters, and outpatient visits.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Among 4232 cases and 19,775 controls with median age of 54 years, adjusted ORs for testing positive for SARS-CoV-2 among those who received BNT162b2 XBB1.5-adapted vaccine a median of 30 days ago (
                    <jats:italic>vs</jats:italic>
                    not having received an XBB1.5-adapted vaccine of any kind) were 0.37 (95% CI: 0.20–0.67) for COVID-19 hospitalization, 0.42 (0.34–0.53) for ED/UC visits, and 0.42 (0.27–0.66) for outpatient visits. Compared to the unvaccinated, those who had received only older versions of COVID-19 vaccines did not show significantly reduced risk of COVID-19 outcomes, including hospital admission.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our findings reaffirm current recommendations for broad age-based use of annually updated COVID-19 vaccines given that (1) XBB1.5-adapted vaccines provided significant additional protection against a range of COVID-19 outcomes and (2) older versions of COVID-19 vaccines offered little, if any, additional protection, including against hospital admission, regardless of the number or type of prior doses received.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>KEY POINTS</jats:title>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Questions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Does receiving the BNT162b2 XBB1.5-adapted vaccine offer additional protection against COVID-19 hospital admission and ambulatory visits in US adults ≥18 years of age compared to not receiving an XBB1.5-adapted vaccine of any kind? Do older versions of COVID-19 vaccine still provide any protection compared to the unvaccinated?</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The BNT162b2 XBB1.5-adapted vaccine (Pfizer-BioNTech 2023–2024 formulation) provided significant additional protection against a range of COVID-19 outcomes during a period when XBB sub-lineages were predominant but JN.1 was also co-circulating and rapidly increasing in prevalence. Older versions of COVID-19 vaccines offered little, if any, additional protection compared to the unvaccinated, including against COVID-19 hospital admission, regardless of the number or type of prior doses received.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our findings reaffirm current recommendations for broad age-based use of annually updated COVID-19 vaccines.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: BNT162b2 XBB1.5-adapted Vaccine and COVID-19 Hospital Admissions and Ambulatory Visits in US Adults
